Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 10   (I. E. Handout 13) (p. 1 of 2)
Ending Relationships
Due Date:             Name:                                    Week Starting:
Fill out this sheet to outline how to end an unwanted relationship when the relationship is not abu-
sive. If it is abusive, first call a local domestic violence hotline or the National Domestic Vio-
lence Hotline (1-800-799-7233). Write on the back of this sheet if you need more room.
Relationship problem: Describe how the relationship is destructive or interfering with your life.


List Wise Mind pros and cons for ending the relationship.
Pros:

Cons:

Script Ideas for DEAR MAN, GIVE FAST to End a Relationship
  1.	Describe the relationship situation, or the problem that is the core reason you want to end the
relationship.
  2.	Express feelings/opinions about why the relationship needs to end for you.
  3.	Assert in your decision to end the relationship directly (circle the part you will use later in
“broken record” to stay mindful if you need it).
  4.	Reinforcing comments to make about positive outcomes for both of you once the relationship
is ended.
(continued on next page)

186
Interpersonal Effectiveness Worksheet 10   (p. 2 of 2)
5.	 Mindful and Appearing confident comments to make about how and when to end (if
needed).
6.	 Negotiating comments to make, plus turn-the-table comments to avoid getting off track and
responding to insults or diversions (if needed).
7.	 Validating comments about the other person’s wishes, feelings, or history of the relationship.
8.	 Easy manner comments.
9.	 Fair comments.
Check off opposite actions for love you have been doing:

 1.  Reminded myself why love is not justified.

 2.  Did the opposite of loving urges.

 3.  Avoided contact with reminders of loved one.

 4.  Other:

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Ending Relationships
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this to protect both people: needs are real, boundaries are kind, and a pause is not abandonment when repair is named.
tiny_steps: Name the ask in one sentence.
Agree on a return time or next check-in.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Use text/chat if speech is hard.
Include caregiver capacity as part of the plan, not an afterthought.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use skills to win, corner, threaten leaving, or make either person prove pain or disability.
